_comment: Decision tree for Modern Panel-Data Causal Inference. Questions are internal nodes; leaves carry method recommendations with chapter deep-links, assumptions, caveats, package, citations, and a minimal code snippet.

leaf-node.title: string
alternatives: string
assumptions: string
caveats: string

q_n_treated.prompt: How many units are ever treated in your panel?
q_n_treated.help: Count units that receive the treatment at any point, not treated unit-periods.

q_few_binary_cts.prompt: Is the treatment binary or continuous?

q_periods.prompt: How many time periods do you observe?

q_2p_binary_cts.prompt: Is the treatment binary or continuous?

q_absorbing.prompt: Is the treatment absorbing (once a unit is treated, it stays treated)?
q_absorbing.help: Absorbing means no switching off. A minimum-wage increase is absorbing; a tax rate that rises and falls is not.

q_staggered.prompt: Do different units adopt treatment at different times (staggered adoption)?

options.label: No, all treated units adopt at the same date

q_binary_cts_staggered.prompt: Is the treatment binary or continuous?

q_covariates_staggered.prompt: Are parallel trends defensible unconditionally, or only after conditioning on covariates?
q_covariates_staggered.help: Pre-treatment / time-invariant covariates that predict differential trends (e.g. baseline income, employment sector, ideology) belong to the second branch and feed into doubly robust DiD. Time-varying confounders — whether observed or unobserved — belong to the third branch and call for an interactive-fixed-effects approach. Post-treatment covariates are bad controls; do not condition on them in any branch.

options.label: Yes — unconditional parallel trends are defensible
No — credible only conditional on pre-treatment covariates
No — credible only after absorbing time-varying confounders

q_stacked_applicable.prompt: Do you want to stay in familiar TWFE syntax while still getting clean never-treated-or-not-yet-treated comparisons?
q_stacked_applicable.help: Stacked DiD (Cengiz et al. 2019; Wing et al. 2024) builds cohort-specific 2×2 sub-panels and stacks them. Requires binary treatment and cohort sizes large enough to support matching windows.

options.label: No — a dedicated heterogeneity-robust estimator is fine

q_binary_cts_single_timing.prompt: Is the treatment binary or continuous?

q_covariates_single_timing.prompt: Are parallel trends defensible unconditionally, or only after conditioning on covariates?
q_covariates_single_timing.help: Same logic as the staggered branch: pre-treatment / time-invariant X feeds DR-DiD; time-varying confounders call for IFE / matrix completion; post-treatment covariates are bad controls.

options.label: Yes — unconditional parallel trends are defensible
No — credible only conditional on pre-treatment covariates
No — credible only after absorbing time-varying confounders

q_binary_cts_nonabs.prompt: Is the treatment binary or continuous?

options.label: Continuous dose that varies over time

q_all_third_dim.prompt: All units are exposed to the treatment at the same time. Do you have an extra source of variation?
q_all_third_dim.help: With no untreated contrast, DiD is not identified unless you have either (a) a baseline factor that determines how intensely each unit is exposed, or (b) an ineligible subgroup that the treatment cannot affect.

options.label: A baseline factor varies exposure across units (e.g. pre-shock intensity)
An ineligible subgroup exists (e.g. age cutoff, jurisdiction, sector)

leaf_scm.title: Synthetic control family
alternatives: Augmented SCM (`augsynth`) — for imperfect pre-fit
Synthetic DiD (`synthdid`) — combines donor weights with DiD's additive-shift robustness
`scpi` — prediction intervals / uncertainty quantification for SCM
assumptions: Stable factor structure across donors and treated unit
Adequate pre-treatment fit between synthetic and actual outcomes
No anticipation
Donor pool not itself affected by the treatment
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: Placebo inference is the classical default; `scpi` offers proper prediction intervals
`augsynth` is the right choice when convex-hull SCM cannot match pre-period
leaf_scm.code:
library(Synth)
# See ?dataprep; then synth(data.prep.obj) for weights and path()

leaf_synthdid_few.title: Synthetic DiD (few treated units)
alternatives: `augsynth` or `scpi` with staggered-adoption option
`fect` (IFE mode) if baseline factors are time-varying
assumptions: Low-rank latent-factor structure
Adequate donor pool
No anticipation
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: With very few treated units, inference relies on placebo permutation
Time and unit weights together soften sensitivity to level shifts
leaf_synthdid_few.code:
library(synthdid)
setup <- panel.matrices(df, unit = 'id', time = 'year',
                        outcome = 'y', treatment = 'D')
sdid <- synthdid_estimate(setup$Y, setup$N0, setup$T0)
summary(sdid)

leaf_fect_few_cts.title: Continuous-dose treatment with few treated units
leaf_fect_few_cts.primary_method: Callaway, Goodman-Bacon & Sant'Anna (2024) `did` continuous branch, applied cohort by cohort. With 2–9 treated units the `ATT(d|d)` level curve is the main estimand; the `ATT'(d|d)` marginal curve is rarely well-powered
alternatives: `synthdid` with a binarized dose (above/below median) as a robustness check
assumptions: Level effects `ATT(d|d)`: weak parallel trends — untreated potential outcomes evolve in parallel across dose levels
Strong PT for the marginal curve — often too strong with few treated units; report only the level curve if so
No anticipation of the treatment or its dose
Overlap across doses in the covariate and outcome support
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: `fect` and related counterfactual-panel estimators assume a binary treatment — they are only appropriate here if you binarize the dose or compare dose levels
Report the full `ATT(d|d)` level curve, not a single scalar; add the `ATT'(d|d)` marginal curve when strong parallel trends is defensible
Few treated units + continuous dose is a thin corner; report diagnostics heavily and consider dose bins
leaf_fect_few_cts.code:
# Callaway/Goodman-Bacon/Sant'Anna (2024) continuous-treatment workflow,
# applied cohort by cohort. With only 2–9 treated units, inference is thin —
# pair with dose-binned synthetic control as a sanity check.

leaf_classic_did.title: Classic 2×2 DiD
leaf_classic_did.primary_method: Two-period TWFE. In this case, it is exactly equivalent to first-difference OLS and non-parametric difference-in-means.
alternatives: Doubly Robust DiD (`DRDID`) if you want to condition on covariates
Non-parametric difference-in-means (exactly equivalent)
assumptions: Parallel trends in untreated potential outcomes
SUTVA (no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants)
No anticipation in the pre-period
caveats: With 2 periods and binary treatment, TWFE and first-differences are algebraically equivalent
Consider `HonestDiD` (Rambachan & Roth) for sensitivity bounds on the parallel-trends assumption
leaf_classic_did.code:
library(fixest)
feols(y ~ post_treat | id + time, data = df)

leaf_continuous_2p.title: Continuous-dose 2×2 DiD
leaf_continuous_2p.primary_method: Callaway, Goodman-Bacon & Sant'Anna (2024) `did` continuous branch. Report the level effect `ATT(d|d)` (dose $d$ vs no treatment, weak PT) and, when strong PT is defensible, the marginal effect `ATT'(d|d)` (dose-response derivative)
alternatives: Dose-binned 2×2 DiD as a descriptive first pass
Smoothed `ATT(d|d)` curve via kernel regression of 2×2 contrasts on dose
assumptions: Level effects `ATT(d|d)`: weak parallel trends — untreated potential outcomes evolve in parallel across dose levels
Marginal / dose-response effects `ATT'(d|d)`: strong parallel trends — the counterfactual trend is invariant to the realized dose
No anticipation of the treatment or its dose
Overlap across doses in the covariate and outcome support
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: A TWFE coefficient on a `post × dose` interaction is neither the level nor the marginal effect under heterogeneity — it is a dose-weighted average whose weights can be negative
Report the full `ATT(d|d)` level curve, not a single scalar; add the `ATT'(d|d)` marginal curve when strong parallel trends is defensible
With only two periods, the level and marginal curves are noisy — report pointwise and uniform confidence bands
leaf_continuous_2p.code:
# Continuous-treatment DiD following Callaway, Goodman-Bacon, Sant'Anna (2024)
# See https://pedrohcgs.github.io/posts/ for the canonical workflow

leaf_twfe_single_timing.title: Two-way fixed effects with common treatment timing
leaf_twfe_single_timing.primary_method: TWFE DiD and event-study around the common adoption date with unit and time fixed effects are the simplest estimator, although any other heterogeneity-robust estimators work too.
alternatives: Doubly Robust DiD if covariates support conditional parallel trends
assumptions: Parallel trends in untreated outcomes
No anticipation
SUTVA
caveats: With common timing and binary treatment TWFE is unbiased under parallel trends — heterogeneity-robust estimators are not strictly required
If any units adopt at different times, move to the staggered branch
leaf_twfe_single_timing.code:
library(fixest)
feols(y ~ i(time, treat, ref = t_treat - 1) | id + time, data = df)

leaf_continuous_single_timing.title: Continuous-dose DiD with common treatment timing
leaf_continuous_single_timing.primary_method: Callaway, Goodman-Bacon & Sant'Anna (2024) `did` continuous branch. Report the level curve `ATT(d|d)` under weak PT; overlay the marginal curve `ATT'(d|d)` when strong PT is defensible
alternatives: Dose-binned event study around the common adoption date
Parametric dose × post interaction (descriptive only — see caveat)
assumptions: Level effects `ATT(d|d)`: weak parallel trends — untreated potential outcomes evolve in parallel across dose levels
Marginal / dose-response effects `ATT'(d|d)`: strong parallel trends — the counterfactual trend is invariant to the realized dose
No anticipation of the treatment or its dose
Overlap across doses in the covariate and outcome support
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: A TWFE coefficient on a `post × dose` interaction is neither the level nor the marginal effect under heterogeneity — it is a dose-weighted average whose weights can be negative
Report the full `ATT(d|d)` level curve, not a single scalar; add the `ATT'(d|d)` marginal curve when strong parallel trends is defensible
Pair the estimate with a dose-specific pre-trend plot (analogue of the binary event-study pre-period)
leaf_continuous_single_timing.code: # Callaway, Goodman-Bacon, Sant'Anna (2024) continuous-treatment DiD workflow

leaf_cs.title: Callaway & Sant'Anna group-time ATT
alternatives: Borusyak, Jaravel & Spiess imputation (`didimputation`)
Sun & Abraham interaction-weighted event study (`fixest::sunab`)
Wooldridge ETWFE (`etwfe`)
de Chaisemartin & D'Haultfœuille DID_l (`DIDmultiplegt_dyn`)
assumptions: Parallel trends in untreated outcomes across cohorts
No anticipation
SUTVA
Absorbing treatment
caveats: Plain TWFE is biased under staggered adoption with heterogeneous effects — use a heterogeneity-robust estimator by default
Choose aggregation (dynamic, group, simple) deliberately — they answer different questions
leaf_cs.code:
library(did)
att <- att_gt(yname = 'y', tname = 'year', idname = 'id',
              gname = 'first_treat', data = df)
aggte(att, type = 'dynamic')

leaf_stacked.title: Stacked DiD
leaf_stacked.primary_method: Stacked DiD (Cengiz et al. 2019; Wing, Freedman & Hollingsworth 2024 weighting correction)
alternatives: Callaway & Sant'Anna group-time ATT
Borusyak, Jaravel & Spiess imputation
assumptions: Parallel trends in untreated outcomes within each matched cohort window
No anticipation
Binary treatment
Absorbing treatment
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: Binary-only — no continuous-treatment variant exists in the current literature
Wing et al. (2024) correction is required to avoid implicit weighting bias
Needs cohort sizes large enough to support per-cohort matching windows
leaf_stacked.code:
# 1. For each treated cohort g, build a sub-panel of cohort g + its
# clean controls (never-treated or not-yet-treated) re-indexed to event time.
# 2. Stack all sub-panels, estimate TWFE on the stacked data.
library(fixest)
feols(y ~ treat_post | id_cohort + time_cohort, data = stacked)

leaf_cs_covariates.title: Callaway & Sant'Anna with conditional parallel trends
alternatives: `DRDID`::drdid directly on each cohort-time 2×2
`PanelMatch` with covariate matching
assumptions: Conditional parallel trends given covariates
No anticipation
Overlap / common support on covariates
Correct specification of at least one of propensity or outcome model (doubly robust)
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: Covariates must be pre-treatment or time-invariant — post-treatment covariates are bad controls
Pair with `HonestDiD` sensitivity analysis
leaf_cs_covariates.code:
library(did)
att <- att_gt(yname = 'y', tname = 'year', idname = 'id',
              gname = 'first_treat', xformla = ~ x1 + x2,
              data = df, est_method = 'dr')
aggte(att, type = 'dynamic')

leaf_fect_ife.title: Interactive fixed effects — fect / synthetic DiD / trajectory balancing
leaf_fect_ife.primary_method: `fect` with interactive fixed effects (IFE) or matrix completion (MC). Latent factors absorb unit-specific trends and time-varying confounders that are correlated with treatment timing.
alternatives: Synthetic DiD (`synthdid`) — unit and time weights soften sensitivity to time-varying shocks
Trajectory balancing (`tjbal`) — reweights to match entire pre-treatment outcome / covariate trajectories rather than just baseline levels
`gsynth` — now a wrapper around `fect`
assumptions: Low-rank latent-factor structure on untreated outcomes
Adequate pre-treatment span to identify the factor loadings
No anticipation
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: Conditioning on observed time-varying X that is itself affected by treatment is a bad-control problem; latent-factor models avoid this by absorbing the confounding rather than conditioning on it
`fect` is diagnostic-heavy — plot residuals, equivalence test, and placebo-in-time before reading the ATT
Synthetic DiD shines with few treated units; with many treated cohorts, `fect` is the natural choice
leaf_fect_ife.code:
library(fect)
fit <- fect(Y ~ D, data = df, index = c('id','year'),
            method = 'ife', force = 'two-way', se = TRUE)
plot(fit, type = 'equiv')           # pre-trend equivalence test
plot(fit, type = 'counterfactual')

leaf_dr_did_single_timing.title: Doubly robust DiD with common timing
leaf_dr_did_single_timing.primary_method: Doubly robust DiD (`DRDID`) applied to the 2×2 around the common adoption date. Reduces to Sant'Anna & Zhao's DR estimator at each event time.
alternatives: `fixest::feols(y ~ i(time, treat, ref = t_treat - 1) + x | id + time)` — TWFE event study with additive covariates (parametric, not doubly robust)
`did::att_gt(xformla = ~ x)` — works under common timing too; doubly robust by default with `est_method = 'dr'`
assumptions: Conditional parallel trends given pre-treatment covariates
No anticipation
Overlap / common support on covariates
Correct specification of at least one of propensity or outcome model (doubly robust)
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: Covariates must be pre-treatment or strictly time-invariant — post-treatment X is a bad control
TWFE-with-covariates is parametric and breaks under interaction between covariates and time; DR-DiD avoids this
Pair with `HonestDiD` sensitivity analysis
leaf_dr_did_single_timing.code:
library(DRDID)
# panel data, common adoption at t_treat
dr <- drdid(yname = 'y', tname = 'year', idname = 'id',
            dname = 'treat', xformla = ~ x1 + x2,
            data = df, panel = TRUE)
summary(dr)

leaf_continuous_staggered.title: Continuous-dose DiD with staggered adoption
leaf_continuous_staggered.primary_method: Callaway, Goodman-Bacon & Sant'Anna (2024) `did` continuous branch, cohort × dose. Report `ATT(d|d, g, t)` level curves under weak PT and the corresponding marginal curves `ATT'(d|d, g, t)` under strong PT
alternatives: Cohort-specific dose-binned event studies
de Chaisemartin & D'Haultfœuille continuous variant in `DIDmultiplegt_dyn`
assumptions: Level effects `ATT(d|d)`: weak parallel trends — untreated potential outcomes evolve in parallel across dose levels and across cohorts
Marginal / dose-response effects `ATT'(d|d)`: strong parallel trends — the counterfactual trend is invariant to the realized dose and across cohorts
No anticipation of the treatment or its dose
Overlap across doses in the covariate and outcome support, including within each cohort
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: Stacked DiD, fuzzy DiD, and most DID-IV variants have no binary-free analogue here

A TWFE coefficient on a `post × dose` interaction is neither the level nor the marginal effect under heterogeneity — it is a dose-weighted average whose weights can be negative — this is especially severe with staggered adoption
Report cohort heterogeneity in the level and marginal curves separately

leaf_fect_nonabs.title: Non-absorbing binary treatment — fect / PanelMatch / DIDmultiplegt_dyn
leaf_fect_nonabs.primary_method: `fect` (IFE or matrix completion) for model-based counterfactuals, or `DIDmultiplegt_dyn` (DID_M / DID_l) for non-parametric switcher aggregation
alternatives: `PanelMatch` (Imai, Kim & Wang) for matching on treatment histories
`gsynth` (now a wrapper around `fect`)
assumptions: Low-rank outcome structure (`fect` IFE/MC) OR sequential ignorability on histories (`PanelMatch`) OR parallel trends between switchers and stable units (`DIDmultiplegt_dyn`)
Overlap in treatment histories
No anticipation
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: Callaway/Sant'Anna and Borusyak-style imputation assume absorption and are inappropriate when treatment switches off
`fect` is diagnostic-heavy — plot residuals and placebo tests before reading the ATT
leaf_fect_nonabs.code:
library(fect)
fit <- fect(Y ~ D, data = df, index = c('id','year'),
            method = 'ife', se = TRUE)
plot(fit, type = 'counterfactual')

leaf_fect_nonabs_cts.title: Non-absorbing continuous treatment — research frontier
leaf_fect_nonabs_cts.primary_method: `DIDmultiplegt_dyn` continuous-treatment option. Focus on the level contrast `ATT(d|d)` at observed doses; marginal effects `ATT'(d|d)` require strong PT that is rarely defensible with switchers
alternatives: Case-by-case reframing as dose-binned switchers
Binarize the dose (e.g. above/below median) and use `fect` for a robustness check — `fect` itself cannot consume a continuous treatment
assumptions: Level effects `ATT(d|d)`: weak parallel trends — untreated potential outcomes evolve in parallel across dose levels, across dose-change trajectories
Marginal / dose-response effects `ATT'(d|d)`: strong parallel trends — the counterfactual trend is invariant to the realized dose — generally implausible under reversals
No anticipation of the treatment or its dose
Overlap in doses both across and within switchers
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: This corner is genuinely thin in the 2026 literature — expect to justify the estimand (level or marginal?) carefully
`fect` and other counterfactual-panel estimators assume binary treatment; they are not primary tools for continuous non-absorbing dose
Document the exact dose definition and timing in the paper; do not rely on package defaults alone
leaf_fect_nonabs_cts.code:
# Non-absorbing + continuous is research-frontier. Start with:
library(DIDmultiplegt)
# did_multiplegt_dyn(df, 'y', 'id', 'year', 'D', ...)

leaf_factorial_did.title: Factorial DiD
leaf_factorial_did.primary_method: Factorial DiD exploiting cross-sectional baseline factor × time interaction
alternatives: Intensity-of-treatment DDD if the baseline factor is ordinal
Shift-share / Bartik-style instrumented DiD if the factor maps to exposure shares
assumptions: Baseline factor is pre-determined and exogenous to the treatment timing
Exclusion restriction: the factor affects outcomes only through the treatment channel
Parallel trends across levels of the factor
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: When every unit is exposed, plain DiD is not identified — you are relying entirely on the factor for identifying variation
No dedicated R package; build from `fixest::feols` using factor × post interactions
leaf_factorial_did.code:
library(fixest)
# Factor × post interaction identifies the moderation effect
feols(y ~ i(post, factor_level, ref = 0) | id + time, data = df)

leaf_ddd.title: Triple differences (DDD)
alternatives: Three-way interaction TWFE (only valid with no covariates and no staggered adoption)
Cohort-level stacked DDD (manual construction)
assumptions: Biases in the two nested 2×2 DiDs cancel (not two separate parallel-trends assumptions)
Eligible / ineligible partition is pre-determined and stable
No spillovers from eligible to ineligible group
SUTVA: no spillovers, stable units, no hidden treatment variants
caveats: Three-way interaction TWFE can fail under covariates or staggered adoption — use `triplediff`'s DDD-specific estimators
The estimand changes from ATT to eligible-subgroup ATT or effect modification depending on the design
leaf_ddd.code:
library(triplediff)
# triplediff estimator on a DDD design
td <- tddid(y ~ treat * post * eligible | id + time, data = df)
summary(td)

leaf_not_identified.title: Design not identified by DiD
leaf_not_identified.primary_method: None — DiD requires an untreated contrast
alternatives: Interrupted time series (ITS) for a pre/post description
Synthetic control if you can construct a donor pool from outside your sample
Layer on extra leverage: DiD+RDD (cutoff), DID-IV (instrument), DDD (ineligible subgroup), factorial DiD (baseline factor)
assumptions: No DiD assumptions suffice here — there is no untreated trend to borrow
caveats: A naïve before/after comparison is not a DiD — it is a time-series trend
If you genuinely have no untreated contrast and no external leverage, DiD is not the right framework
leaf_not_identified.code: # No DiD estimator. See Chapter 7 on hybrid designs (DiD+RDD, DID-IV, DDD, factorial DiD).
